::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
```{=latex}
\clearpage
\thispagestyle{empty}
\begin{minipage}[t][0.88\textheight][t]{\textwidth}

\vspace{2cm}

{\fontsize{16}{20}\selectfont\sffamily\bfseries Propositions}

\vspace{0.6cm}
{\color{thesissand}\rule{\textwidth}{1.5pt}}
\vspace{0.8cm}

\begin{enumerate}[label={\bfseries\arabic*.}]
  \setlength{\itemsep}{1.2em}

  \item Structural complexity is essential for the recovery of kōura populations — it 
        is not just an added benefit but a fundamental requirement.\\
        \hspace*{1em}{\small\itshape (this thesis)}

  \item The fear of predators shapes prey behaviour as powerfully as predation
        itself, yet habitat restoration rarely accounts for it.\\
        \hspace*{1em}{\small\itshape (this thesis)}

  \item Rock pile deployment is a viable and scalable tool for freshwater crayfish
        restoration in lakes, but success depends on location and design, not just presence.\\
        \hspace*{1em}{\small\itshape (this thesis)}
        
  \item Freshwater crayfish are cute animals and very important for ecosystems.

  \item Scientists write, review, and edit research for free, then pay to publish
        it and pay again to read it - the academic publishing industry is the
        most successful business scam in modern knowledge production.

  \item Scientific knowledge is a public good. It should be openly accessible, 
        transparently evaluated, and preserved in a way that keeps it useful and verifiable 
        — not just today, but across generations.



\end{enumerate}

\end{minipage}

\vfill

{\color{thesissand}\rule{\textwidth}{0.8pt}}
\vspace{0.4cm}

{\small\sffamily
Propositions belonging to the thesis, entitled\\[0.3em]
\textit{Restoring native freshwater crayfish habitat in invaded lake ecosystems}\\[0.3em]
Olivier V. Raven\\
The University of Waikato, 2026
}
\clearpage
```
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
### Propositions {.unnumbered}

**From this thesis**

1. Structural complexity is essential for the recovery of kōura populations — it is not just an added benefit but a fundamental requirement.
2. The fear of predators shapes prey behaviour as powerfully as predation itself, yet habitat restoration rarely accounts for it.
3. Rock pile deployment is a viable and scalable tool for freshwater crayfish restoration in lakes, but success depends on location and design, not just presence.

**On science and publishing**

4. Freshwater crayfish are cute animals and very important for ecosystems.
5. Scientists write, review, and edit research for free, then pay to publish it and pay again to read it - the academic publishing industry is the most successful business scam in modern knowledge production.
6. Scientific knowledge is a public good. It should be openly accessible, transparently evaluated, and preserved in a way that keeps it useful and verifiable — not just today, but across generations.
:::
